text.title: Scope Drift Metrics — Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules every scope-drift dashboard must satisfy. The rules guarantee the metrics catch real drift instead of producing alarm fatigue or false comfort.

rule.statement: The dashboard MUST compute all five named metrics nightly. Cherry-picking a subset is forbidden because each metric catches a different drift pattern.

rationale.source: PMI scope-performance research; field experience with single-metric dashboards

rule.rationale: CR-volume catches explicit changes. AC-rewrite catches sneaky changes inside accepted tickets. Story-split catches estimation drift. Churn ratio catches add-and-delete cycles. Parking-lot age catches the demo-creep backlog. A dashboard missing any one has a blind spot the project will eventually fall into.

rule.statement: Thresholds (green / amber / red) MUST be expressed relative to the signed baseline (e.g. "AC-rewrite rate exceeds 15% of baseline AC count per sprint"), not absolute. Absolute thresholds bias the dashboard toward project size.

rationale.source: ratio-based performance measurement; PMBOK earned-value practice

rule.rationale: Absolute thresholds (e.g. "≥20 CRs is red") under-warn a large project (which can absorb 20) and over-warn a small one (where 5 is catastrophic). Baseline-relative thresholds work across project sizes.

rule.statement: Dashboard refresh cadence MUST be ≤24h. Weekly refresh is insufficient because drift accumulates within a single sprint.
rule.rationale: By the time a weekly-refresh dashboard shows red, the team has already done a week of work on uncontested-but-creep tickets. Daily refresh detects the inflection within the sprint.

rule.statement: Each amber and red threshold has a named trigger action with owner and SLA. "Increase awareness" is not an action. Red triggers always route through `risk-threshold-policy`.

rationale.source: incident-management practice; field experience with dashboards-without-actions

rule.rationale: A dashboard without actions is decoration. The named action turns each threshold breach into a calendar event with an owner; the SLA prevents the action from being indefinitely deferred.

rule.statement: The dashboard has exactly one named owner who reviews it daily and acknowledges amber/red signals. Unowned dashboards are forbidden.
rule.rationale: The owner converts data into decisions. Without an owner, a red signal sits visible but unacted-upon for days. The acknowledgement event also produces the audit trail for later disputes.

list.item: CR-volume velocity: count of tickets classified as CR (per `scope-change-vs-scope-creep-detection`) divided by baseline-requirement count, per sprint. Green ≤3%, amber 3-8%, red >8%.
AC-rewrite rate: count of acceptance-criteria edits AFTER sprint start, divided by AC count for the sprint's tickets. Green ≤5%, amber 5-15%, red >15%.
Story-split count: number of stories split mid-sprint divided by sprint story count. Green ≤5%, amber 5-15%, red >15%.
Requirement-churn ratio: (added + deleted requirements over 14 days) ÷ total baseline requirements. Green ≤2%, amber 2-8%, red >8%.
Parking-lot age: median age (hours) of OPEN parking-lot items. Green ≤24h, amber 24-72h, red >72h.

example.title: Amber on AC-rewrite rate triggers an early conversation
example.code: Sprint 5, day 6: AC-rewrite rate = 11% (amber threshold). Trigger action: BA pulls the affected tickets; finds 3 stories with rewritten AC all related to the same module (reporting). Conversation with PM: "reporting module has unclear spec, not creep". Re-baseline conversation scheduled within 5 days, change-control input drafted. No surprise at sprint review.
example.note: Illustrates r1 (metric measured), r3 (caught mid-sprint), r4 (named trigger), r5 (BA owned).

example.title: The dashboard nobody reads
example.code: Dashboard exists; AC-rewrite at 22% (red) for 3 weeks; nobody acknowledged. Sprint 8 review: 40% of stories not actually meeting AC; client refuses to sign off; full re-baseline triggered with steering committee involvement.
example.note: r4 (no trigger action followed) and r5 (no owner) violated. The dashboard saw the drift; the project did not.
